speaker: interviewee
text: --was moving around. Maybe her clogs were fitting better than mine. And one day, she came up to me and she said, they're looking for people to send to work. And I asked her, how do you know it's to work and not around the block to the crematoria. She said, I have seen them given bread. And even to my numb mind, the thought penetrated that the Germans would not waste bread on somebody that has to go to a crematoria. And then she obviously registered it. I must tell you that there was no communication whatsoever between people and the thing that helped you in your survival, it helped, helped you to maintain the idea that once you wear a human-- was if you knew somebody who knew your name and at night could call your name. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your, your girlfriend--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was my girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she registered both of you to go to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And one day, they called our number. And were put in cattle trucks. And were given a double portion of bread. And I cannot tell you after how many days and after how many nights we landed in Ravensbruck. But if you imagine that Auschwitz was here and Ravensbruck was there on the top of Germany, Ravensbruck was a sterile experience. It was cold. It was blue. People were not human. Our blockal-- it was a-- our blockalteste was lesbian, alcoholic, Polish, antisemite.

speaker: interviewer
text: The lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lot, in one person. But our stubenalteste-- and this is why I maintain all the time that humanity did not die during the war. Our stubenalteste was a lady from Polish nobility. She had a hyphenated name, which was only in Polish nobility. Her first name was Maria.

speaker: interviewer
text: She treated us like the most precious creatures on God's earth. She gave us water and food and attended to our wounds. And I asked her, [INAUDIBLE] Maria, do you like Jews? She said, not at all, but first, you are human beings. Which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a while-- I don't know, maybe we were six weeks in Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? What work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go to quarries. The work was not work in the sense of work. The work was devised as torture and as demeaning-- demeaning you even further. Because if you do something what is useless, you yourself are useless.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's-- we were pushed to a quarry. And it was wet. And it was cold. And for the first time, it occurred to me that I won't survive, I won't be able to survive that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did your girlfriend pull you through that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: Instinct?

speaker: interviewee
text: --what [INAUDIBLE]. We, we never worked together, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: But didn't you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They always separated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But didn't you at night interact with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only remember that thought. I only remember that thought. Probably the only act that humanized me at that time was that [INAUDIBLE] Maria. Where we talked Polish. We talked about the old times. She explained to me the difference of being antisemite that is against the Jews spoiling the Polish economy, which means economic boycotts, yes, but physical violence, no. And she said to me that she does not hope for Jews to return to Poland after the war, not that she believed that we will survive, but the one who will survive. Anyhow, but that was the humanizing factor that everywhere was. And we were sent again to a fantastic place. It was a salt mine, this used salt mine, into which a munition factory was transferred somewhere from Berlin. And this munition factory produced rockets that were supposed to fly pilotless over London-- VW-2-- [GERMAN]. And we were put to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, each one did something else. I was put some nuts and bolts. The place was a salt mine. The formation of the salt rocks was fantastic. A whole hall, a floor-- we were 600 meters underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: A hole, a salt mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was it cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warm. Inside the belly of the earth, nature earth. Nature protected you again. And it was warm. And was-- the aura of cleanness was there. The bluish whiteness of this formation of rocks, the play of the light. The-- you know, like a prism. Lights were jumping from one corner to another playing. The rising stars sent rays and colors. It was-- and in between were rows and rows of gleaming machinery. And when you stood above that-- because we had to descend, and we saw it from above-- the people looked like ants.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working in some sort of a machine. The civilian-- civilian Germans were with us. The SS did not have an authority over us while at work. But three times a day, they had the authority. That was in the morning to take us to the toilet, lunch time to bring us our soup, and in the afternoon to take us in to the toilet. We left our compound when it was dark. And we returned when it was dark. We usually left our compound about 5 o'clock in the morning. And we returned about 10, 11 o'clock at night. So if there was a township, they didn't know that there are people there. We were not seen from above.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you said you were given food in the middle of the day. When you came back to the camp, did you again receive food?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I say food--

speaker: interviewer
text: Liquid.

speaker: interviewee
text: --do not have images of hot, thick soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Liquid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Liquid.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a touch of warmth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Depends how long it took them to bring it to us in the middle of the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the number that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were surrounded by civilian Germans who cherished their work very highly, because the alternative was a front. My meister was a German who was punished for help once for helping a prisoner. And he was sent to Gross-Rosen. And when he came from Gross-Rosen, and he saw what is done there, nobody wanted to talk to him. And he didn't want to talk to them. He was always isolated. And when I came to work and I am using normal words and we were stationed behind the machine, he used to come behind me and say, be careful when you take out the tools from your locker-- there is something for you. And indeed, it could have been a sandwich, half a sandwich, a hard-boiled egg, a small piece of soap, anything that I could take with me to the camp. It never occurred to me that I have to eat it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took it to--

speaker: interviewer
text: You took it to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, naturally. Seshka would have done the same thing, only she didn't have the opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from, from there--

speaker: interviewee
text: From there and about towards the end of April, he always told me, make sure that you are on the same shift that I am. We worked shift work. And one day, my shift was told not to go down to the factory. And we again were 'Juden raus,' marched off to cattle trucks. We were told that we are going to dig our own graves, which by that time, we did believe. It was toward the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Willie-- We're talking April, '45. My Willie-- that was the-- his name-- provided me with all the latest bulletin. He says, the Russians are 50 kilometers from Berlin, this and this. And I was the walking radio. And we knew that it is the end of the war very clear, very near. Germans lost the war. There was no doubt about it. And the one who worked in those factories, old and young, were taken into the army. And a few civilians who were the meisters and still worked on bombs, on ammunition, cherished their job above Hitler's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken on cattle trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were cattle-- taken on cattle trucks. We didn't know where. Anyhow, whatever it was, we were never on a death march. It was spring. We were taken in open cattle trucks. I remember we passed Hamburg. And for the first time, I have seen a bombed city. We were standing each other on-- on each other's shoulders. People were trampled to death. We didn't have food. We didn't have water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you traveling approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows how long?

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they-- but there was one redeeming--

speaker: interviewer
text: Feature?

speaker: interviewee
text: --feature. A German guard was with us in the cab-- in the wagon. And as long as there was a German, we knew that we won't be gassed. The other redeeming feature is that it was roofless. And one day, the, the train stopped. It was never advised to station as a train that carries people, always as a goods train. And there were never preparations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, the train stopped because-- the rumors went that they had to give the engine to a troops train. They took-- they didn't have rails. They didn't have enough carriages to transport wounded soldiers. But they had all the facilities to transport Jews to nowhere. Where was there to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were told to get out and stand in a quadrangle facing each other, not to turn around. And in four corners were machine guns positioned. It was a beautiful spring day. I saw green grass for the first time. There were just buds on the trees. It was not a pine tree, but a forest, very gentle, very soft. We couldn't look to the left. We couldn't look to the right, only in front. And what I saw, I saw tired faces, frightened, a reflection of my own feelings, and German machine guns. And I said, it is impossible. It cannot happen, not now. And suddenly, it was a burst of laughter. And we were told to get back to our wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a joke?

speaker: interviewee
text: They cleaned-- listen. They had to clean the wagons. They couldn't allow one single German to sit in that stink.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we-- as I said, we must have been somewhere close to Hamburg. We stopped again. Again, we were called 'Juden raus.' And we were pushed somewhere. Was at the station by that time. Nobody cared, really. The-- we were definitely on the German territory. And a lot of Germans-- because by that time, the transports were mixed. German girls started to run away. They were at home. And we were still pushed, pushed, pushed forward. And we were put somewhere in a compound. And they shut the door. And there were two bunks. It must have been a cell. Maybe it was a prison. And within seconds, the bunks were demolished, because, if hundreds, wild, hungry creatures saw a bunk--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dissent.

speaker: interviewee
text: --everybody wanted to have. By that time, we did not operate with any reasonable rules.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was nowhere to sit, nowhere to stand, nowhere to relieve yourself, nobody to talk to. There was screaming, animal screaming. We were still this tight group of people who still spoke a human language. Within hours, we were knee-high in our own excrement and urine. There were no buckets. We all suffered from continuous diarrhea. What could you do? Once, they tried to open the door. And the whole flood of the excrement rushed out. And they quickly shut the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow-- I don't know whether it was a day or a night, an hour or two days-- again, 'Juden raus.' And they opened the door. And the flood of stinks flew out. And they said, 'Juden raus.' And when there is 'Juden raus,' there is only mean one thing-- bad for Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw buses, ambulances, with a red cross. And we-- by that time, we were all so [INAUDIBLE]. We knew that when it was a red cross, this is a gassing van. There's no other way. And men in strange uniforms, speaking a strange language-- they looked at us--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they said, 'Juden raus.'

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. The Germans were still around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the men didn't say anything. But the men wore a uniform that we have never seen. And they showed us with a gesture to enter the buses. And we stood there like a brick wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in an open field somewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it must have been a courtyard somewhere. And they all had armbands with a red cross. But you couldn't [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, no. Not at that stage of the war. We didn't budge. The men were whispering among themselves. And finally, somebody approached a guard and asked, obviously, for an explanation. And maybe the guard gave them an explanation reluctantly. This is the instinct of life. We still did not want to move because we thought it's a gassing van. And they understood we think it's a gassing van. So they got up. They went into the van. They closed the door. After five minutes, they opened the door and, with a broad smile, made us welcoming gesture-- not to us. We made them understand that they have to run the motor. By that time, each of us was an accomplished technician. Gas is distributed by motor. They were completely baffled. The Germans came forward with the explanation. One went into the driver cabin, started the motor. They went, again, into the van, closed the door. And when then, they emerged alive, then-- only then-- we went. And we went into these buses. These buses transferred us God knows where.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who--

speaker: interviewee
text: We still did not know who they-- we were told that that was the International Red Cross. We were brought to a station. And I was in a group to-- that was sent with an old army blanket to fetch bread. The station was full of German soldiers. And they were jumping from joy, kissing each other, hugging each other. And I thought they won the war-- crying, throwing their guns in the air. And I said, my God. They won the war. And I edged myself, with the three girls in tow, to the group. They all talked Plattdeutsche, a different thing. And I understood only two words, [GERMAN], he's dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did we rush back with that bread? The bread was spinning on the platform. And we said-- and I told them what I heard. And then came an old guard that was sitting with us in the train. When I say old, we were 20. He must've been 54, 55, or 60. And he was from the Wehrmacht. He was not suitable for the front any more. He came into the wagon-- to the cattle truck-- and said to us, [SPEAKING GERMAN], my children, I am going now where you came from. And he was crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, civilian population found out that there's a train with prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see where the station was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The station was advised that's a goods train coming. And they came. And they started to throw food to us. They were not allowed to get near the train. So they threw food. And whoever grabbed the food had the food. And I have grabbed a packet of biscuits. And again, it didn't occur to us. We're four or five. And I said to them girls, let's give him this packet of biscuits. If he's going where we came from, we all understood. He will need food. We are going towards the world of life, of plenty. And we gave him the packet of biscuits. Five girls-- there was not one objection. None of us thought differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he left us. And we felt so abandoned. We felt unprotected. Who will tell us what do? You couldn't think for yourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? For five, six years, you were told when to sleep and when to wake up, where to go and how to walk, and what to eat and what to do. You are deprived of a normal human initiative. You are deprived of the power of decisions. There was a, a life with no choice. There was a life where you did not have to make decisions because there was no alternative to it. And suddenly, when they said to us, could you tell us a story, and we have a favorite story, that one day we'll sleep in a clean bed-- each one in her own. We'll have a [INAUDIBLE], a warm [INAUDIBLE]. And on the table will be a whole, round, floury, fresh, warm bread and a glass of hot chocolate. And the train started to move. And we were without protection. And we really felt without protection. We must have been in Jutland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went-- the train went?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we stopped. Then they unloaded us. And they put us, for the night, to a barn. And there we heard airplanes. For the first time, we didn't want a bomb to drop. And they gave us food. There were-- it must have been a big farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what date are we talking now? Because the war had finished, and they were still taking you deeper into Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war finished on the eighth of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of May.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the end of--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of April.

speaker: interviewee
text: --of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- they took-- we were on the border of Jutland. Jutland was Denmark. When we eventually arrived to Denmark, Germans were still occupying Denmark. We showed them our treasures, our sandwiches that were squashed and smashed and torn and wrapped up in paper, but not eaten. We teased them that we have got food and they have nothing. And somebody from the Danish people came up-- look at the big broad smile. I've got here a Red Cross sign that's not very visible--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: --here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a girl who came to call me, that are giving us soup. We're given plates and spoons. We were on the station in Copenhagen. And here was a young man from the International Red Cross who talk to me. I didn't understand what he says. He didn't have the faintest idea what I am talking about. He was snapping the camera. I cannot tell you how many things. Then he started to write letters to me. He was Danish. Christian was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came to visit me in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we heard, in Sweden, on the cattle--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a second-- from Denmark, you were taken to--?

speaker: interviewee
text: By ferry to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sweden, we were properly taken care of, physically, even food. First of all, having a scrub. They couldn't get us out of the [INAUDIBLE]. Men, women-- we didn't care. We were given rough brushes. We scrubbed ourselves till blood came out. We had plenty of soap. Mind you, each and every one stole their piece of soap. That we learned-- whatever is given to you, you have to steal, because you might not have it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were put in quarantine. By that time was end of April. [INAUDIBLE] I think, was the place. We were given one dress. Ah, I should show you the photograph. I've got it in there. Can he stop the camera for a second?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you stop the camera for a second?

speaker: interviewer
text: --finished. You were in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Officially, the war was finished-- and this is a historical fact-- on the 8th of May. But we were, since the end of April-- for us, when we had our first wash, the war was finished. We saw the last of the German. We were not interested, sadly to say, with the politics. That was the end of the war. Within 48 hours, I knew, because our names were given to the Red Cross-- and they were circulated around the world around the clock. And my family in Palestine read it on those bands that were going around and around, that I am alive. And I heard, on the radio, that they told me that my brother is alive. And of course, my family in Palestine is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you heard that through a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sweden, on the quarantine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew I am not alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that feeling like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a tremendous feeling. Again, I was not alone. I am not a typical survivor because, all throughout the war, I had a support of another human being. And even be it my parents, my friend, or so on. And then we started corresponding. I started to get parcels. And I stayed in Sweden for one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a reason you stayed in Sweden for one year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I was with my friends. And I was-- I stayed with a private Swedish family. I worked. It was not so easy to decide where you want to go. And as I tried to explain before, the power of decision was taken away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kitia--

speaker: interviewee
text: So my family decided the next move. And my family, in Paris-- my brother was in France-- decided that they want to bring me to France. After all, Palestine was not free. The year was '45. And nobody was somehow thinking. First of all, the entry to Palestine was illegal. And I was definitely incapable of doing another--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me--

speaker: interviewee
text: --illegal thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you, when did you find out about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: About?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. In August, I found out that my mother is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And gradually, in, in-- about my father, I found out in February, '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got to Paris, or to France, did you join your aunts and uncles or your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No. My brother lived in the country, being a veterinary surgeon. I spent some time with him. But I lived in Paris. And there I married. And my husband had a brother here. And we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you reunited with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother came to Paris before I left for Australia. And my mother decided-- or again, her family decided-- that she should come to Palestine. And perhaps I will join her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when--

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to Palestine. And I did not join her.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you came out to Australia because your husband had a family here. Was that a good move for you, coming to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from retrospective, from hindsight?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: From hindsight, I perhaps would have move-- made a different move. At this time, A, I was really incapable of independent decision. And B, it was as far from Europe as possible, which fulfilled the wish of my father. And secondly, it was peace and quiet. And that's it. There was no-- I don't think I would've stayed in France. The family that I had in France, after all, I did not know. We were all damaged and scarred by war. And I don't think I would've stayed in France. But these are reflections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: The facts are that I did come to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Australia has, has-- has Australian been good to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want me to finish up with a banner for propaganda? I think we were good for Australia, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a very good citizens-- obedient, appreciative. We gave our children to this country. We gave them education. We were not helped when I came to Australia. We were-- there were no facilities. I don't blame the government, because they did not know what to do with us. We were an unknown quantity to them. They were scared of us-- but not only the Australians, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: As for the generosity of opening their gates-- maybe, if they would've opened them few years before, it would be different. I am grateful but-- that they helped us in the process of healing. I think I paid my debt to Australian society. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think there was a debt that you owed or had to pay to Australian society.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you asked me if Australia was good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Good for you. Yes. Kitia, on behalf of the Visual Shoah Foundation, thank you for sharing the history of your life, the strangeness, the weirdness of experiences, but that diminishing will did come through in the end and saw you through as a survivor. You're strong. And thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTING] My grandfather, Joachim Szpigelman, his wife, I think Hinda Szpigelman, their oldest son, Henry-- who at that time was in Paris-- Jean, my father Moses, his sister Sally, his sister Sophie, his brother Leon, who was also in Benczyn, their sister Bella, their-- oh no. This is George. This is a Jean. And their son Elie, their youngest son Meyer, and their sister Lypi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in 1911?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you please tell us about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Engagement [POINTING] photo of my parents-- my father, my mother Zofia, my father's sister Sally, my father's deceased sister Sophie, who was not on the other one, and his sister-in-law Rachel, taken-- This is grandpa Elie, Jehoshua Hanoch Erlich, the father of my mother, photo taken in 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Kitia?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother with her firstborn son, Aron, photo taken in 1916. He was born in 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: One year old, here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years later or so-- my mother and myself, probably taken in 1922. I was six months old, I was told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Go, please, Kitia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That's myself, my father, my mother, and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Kitia, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the youngest sister of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- her name was Rachel. But she was called and known as Ru. She was a dental surgeon. That her husband, Samuel Cygler, who was an artist who painted. And that is their-- on their honeymoon in Nice. I don't know exactly what year it is. But the next photo is my aunt, her 1-year-old daughter Tamara, and myself. I think that was taken in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is a photo of Tamara at the age of 2, I think. Her father, Samuel Cygler, the sculptor-- I've forgotten his name-- who sculpted the head of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This is the same child, my beautiful [? Maruscha, ?] who was rescued by this Polish woman, Genowefa Pajak. [? Maruscha ?] is here 7 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kitia, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is April, 1983. I am at Yad Vashem planting a tree to honor Gena Pajak as a righteous among nations for saving the life of a Jewish child, my cousin, Tamara Cygler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kitia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most unlikely, but this is me in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: This.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Kitia, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself in 1943. My mother-- my parents probably knew what's in store for us. And they wanted to take a picture of me. And somehow, they smuggled out the picture. And I found it after the war with my family in Paris or in Israel. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very--
